title: PRD: Optimize Quality Guardrails — Mandatory Quality Gating for Config Changes
preview:
## Summary
Redwood Optimize can recommend cost-saving configuration changes (batching, caching, quantization profiles) that can materially affect model outputs. This PRD defines **mandatory quality evaluation gates** that must pass before Optimize can apply any supported config change to production traffic (automati...
text:
## Summary
Redwood Optimize can recommend cost-saving configuration changes (batching, caching, quantization profiles) that can materially affect model outputs. This PRD defines **mandatory quality evaluation gates** that must pass before Optimize can apply any supported config change to production traffic (automatically or via one-click). The intent is to prevent silent quality regressions while keeping developer experience fast via tiered gates (smoke → full) and clear override policy with auditability.

**This PRD is for Hosted API gating enforcement.** Dedicated will follow after GA; Private will be manual-first with documentation and UI surfaces.

---

## Problem
Optimize today can surface recommendations that improve unit economics but may change output quality. Without systematic gating, we rely on post-deploy detection (customer complaints, ad-hoc monitoring) and reactive roll...

text:
## Overview
This document defines Redwood Inference’s **Marketplace SKU Catalog v2** for AWS Marketplace and GCP Marketplace.

The catalog is designed to:
- Map cleanly to Redwood’s internal billing meters and entitlements (no “special marketplace-only plans” in product).
- Support enterprise procurement patterns (usage-based, commits, reserved capacity, and Private licensing).
- Reduce buyer confusion by aligning names across clouds: **Hosted** vs **Dedicated** vs **Private**, plus add-ons.

This is the source of truth for:
- SKU naming conventions
- Inclusions/exclusions
- Marketplace billing dimensions → internal meters
- Entitlement semantics used by provisioning and metering

---

## Scope
**In scope:**
- Hosted API (usage-based) SKUs
- Dedicated capacity (reserved) SKUs
- Private deployment (license) SKUs
- Compliance + observability add-ons sold via marketplace

**Out of scope ...

text:
## 1. Summary
This document specifies the end-to-end onboarding flow for customers purchasing Redwood Inference through AWS Marketplace or GCP Marketplace.

The flow begins at **marketplace subscription/contract acceptance** and ends at **first successful API key creation and first successful request**. It covers:
- Entitlement verification (AWS/GCP)
- Redwood org provisioning and linking to a marketplace identity
- SKU/plan mapping and initial quota/limits
- Console UX and error handling
- Edge cases: existing orgs, multiple subscriptions, delayed entitlement propagation, region/residency constraints

Primary goal: reduce marketplace onboarding friction while preserving correctness for billing/metering and security expectations.

---

## 2. Goals and non-goals
### 2.1 Goals
1) **Time-to-first-success**: minimize time from marketplace purchase to first successful Redwood API request.

title: Q1 2026 Theme: Self‑Serve Activation & Early Retention (Hosted API)
preview:
## Overview
Q1 2026 focuses on improving self‑serve activation and early retention for the **Hosted API** by making the “first successful request” and “first 100k tokens” milestones faster, more reliable, and easier to reach.

This theme connects a set of coordinated initiatives across **lifecycle comms (email + ret...
text:
## Overview
Q1 2026 focuses on improving self‑serve activation and early retention for the **Hosted API** by making the “first successful request” and “first 100k tokens” milestones faster, more reliable, and easier to reach.

This theme connects a set of coordinated initiatives across **lifecycle comms (email + retargeting)**, **console onboarding**, **docs/quickstarts**, and **measurement + handoff signals**. The centerpiece execution vehicle is the 4‑week **Lifecycle Growth Experiments Sprint** (email v2, in‑console nudges, and retargeting), with follow‑on work to graduate winners into always‑on onboarding.

## Why this theme (problem statement)
We are seeing avoidable drop‑off between:
1) signup → 2) API key created → 3) first successful request → 4) sustained usage (D7/D30)

Primary drivers (based on funnel analysis, support tickets, and qualitative feedback):
- Developers miss t...

## Purpose
This page documents how Redwood Inference is packaged and sold via **AWS Marketplace**, including:
- Supported **offer types** (public + private offers)
- **Contract and usage dimensions** used for pricing and billing
- How we implement **AWS metering and entitlements**
- A checklist of **required AWS submission fields and attachments**

This is the source of truth for Partnerships/GTM, Product, Billing Engineering, and Finance/Legal during offer creation and marketplace submissions.

---

## Scope (AWS Marketplace)
Redwood Inference is offered on AWS Marketplace as a **SaaS** product (not AMI or container image distribution).

We support the following purchasing motions:
1) **Public offer** for self-serve usage-based procurement (Hosted).
2) **Private offer** for negotiated terms (committed spend, enterprise add-ons, and Dedicated/Private deployments).

title: PRD: Workload-aware batching defaults (Fast / Standard / Cost-efficient SLO tiers)
preview:
## Summary
Redwood Inference will ship workload-aware batching defaults exposed as three customer-facing latency SLO tiers: **Fast**, **Standard**, and **Cost-efficient**. The platform will recommend and/or apply tiered batching profiles that improve throughput and $/1K tokens while keeping tail latency predictable ...
text:
## Summary
Redwood Inference will ship workload-aware batching defaults exposed as three customer-facing latency SLO tiers: **Fast**, **Standard**, and **Cost-efficient**. The platform will recommend and/or apply tiered batching profiles that improve throughput and $/1K tokens while keeping tail latency predictable and avoiding unsafe scheduler configurations.

This PRD defines customer needs, tier semantics, configuration UX (Hosted/Dedicated/Private), default behavior, observability requirements, rollout plan, and launch criteria tied to business goals.

---

## Problem statement
Batching is one of the highest-leverage knobs for inference unit economics, but manual configuration is:
- Error-prone (tail latency regressions, queue blowups, starvation under mixed prompt lengths)
- Hard to tune across models/hardware and rapidly changing workloads
- Difficult to explain and support (cus...

title: Redwood Private 2026.01 — Signed offline bundles for air-gapped installs
preview:
h2. Summary
Redwood Private 2026.01 introduces *cryptographically signed offline bundles* for *air-gapped and offline* installations and upgrades. The installer now verifies bundle integrity and provenance *before* applying changes to the cluster, reducing supply-chain risk and preventing accidental installation of ...
text:
h2. Summary
Redwood Private 2026.01 introduces *cryptographically signed offline bundles* for *air-gapped and offline* installations and upgrades. The installer now verifies bundle integrity and provenance *before* applying changes to the cluster, reducing supply-chain risk and preventing accidental installation of tampered or incomplete artifacts.

h2. Who this impacts
* You are impacted if you install or upgrade Redwood Private using *offline media* (USB, secure file transfer, offline repository sync) and your environment has *limited or no outbound connectivity*.
* Connected installs are unaffected unless you choose to use the offline bundle workflow.

h2. What’s new
h3. 1) Signed offline bundles (new default for offline installs)
Offline bundles now include:
* A canonical *bundle manifest* listing required artifacts, versions, and hashes.
* A *detached signature* over the canonica...

text:
## Summary
This sprint is a 4-week, cross-functional Growth + Product effort to improve self-serve activation and early retention for the **Hosted API**.

We will run a tightly-instrumented set of experiments across:
- **Lifecycle email:** onboarding drip v2
- **Console:** contextual onboarding nudges (feature-flagged)
- **Paid retargeting:** conservative audiences focused on re-engaging non-activated and early-activated users

This is a measurement-first sprint: we are prioritizing clean event taxonomy, holdouts, and decision criteria to avoid “noisy” learnings.

---

## Sprint goals (what success looks like)
### Primary activation goals
Within **7 days** of signup:
1) Increase **% of new signups completing a first successful request**
2) Increase **% of new signups reaching the first 100k tokens milestone**

### Secondary goals
- Reduce **time-to-first-token (TTFT)** and “time-to-va...

title: PRD: Safe Apply + One-Click Rollback for Optimization Configs
preview:
## Summary
Redwood Inference customers increasingly use Redwood Optimize to tune batching, caching, and quantization settings to improve unit economics. Today, optimization changes are risky because they can cause regressions (latency spikes, elevated 5xx, quality drops) and rollbacks are manual and error-prone.

Th...
text:
## Summary
Redwood Inference customers increasingly use Redwood Optimize to tune batching, caching, and quantization settings to improve unit economics. Today, optimization changes are risky because they can cause regressions (latency spikes, elevated 5xx, quality drops) and rollbacks are manual and error-prone.

This PRD defines a versioned “Optimization Config” object plus a safe-apply workflow with gated progressive rollout and a one-click rollback (Console + API). The feature set targets Hosted + Dedicated first, with an operator-friendly compatibility model and rollback procedure for Private.

## Problem statement
Optimization/config changes are currently treated like “best-effort” toggles and are not consistently:
- Traceable (who changed what, and when)
- Reversible (fast revert to known-good state)
- Guardrailed (canary + SLO/quality gating)
- Enforced via RBAC (who is authori...

title: PRD: Redwood Private — Signed Offline Bundles for Air-Gapped Install/Upgrade
preview:
## Summary
Redwood Private customers with air-gapped environments need a repeatable, verifiable way to install and upgrade without trusting online fetches or ad-hoc checksums. This PRD defines signed offline bundles: a deterministic bundle format, a cryptographic signature produced by Redwood’s release pipeline, and...
text:
## Summary
Redwood Private customers with air-gapped environments need a repeatable, verifiable way to install and upgrade without trusting online fetches or ad-hoc checksums. This PRD defines signed offline bundles: a deterministic bundle format, a cryptographic signature produced by Redwood’s release pipeline, and installer-side verification that fails closed when artifacts are tampered, incomplete, or not issued by a trusted Redwood signing key.

This work reduces supply-chain risk, improves audit readiness for regulated buyers, and standardizes support workflows for offline deployments.

## Problem statement
Today, Redwood Private on-prem/VPC customers who cannot allow outbound network access (or who must transport artifacts via removable media) face several issues:
- Integrity verification is inconsistent: customers may validate a few checksums, but not the full set of artifacts ...

## Overview
Email Onboarding Drip v2 is the primary lifecycle email sequence for new self-serve Hosted API signups. The sequence is designed to reduce time-to-first-token (TTFT), increase early activation (first successful request + first 100k tokens within 7 days), and improve D7/D30 return usage.

This document specifies triggers, segmentation, send schedule, copy variants, compliance requirements, deliverability guardrails, and the measurement / experiment plan.

**Primary audience:** Developers who sign up for Redwood API self-serve and have not yet reached early activation milestones.

**Owners**
- Experiment owner: Ben Carter (Growth/Marketing)
- Messaging review: Soojin Lee
- Marketing ops + attribution: Tyler Benson

title: Status Page Transparency: Console Integration Spec (Incidents + Post‑Incident Summaries)
preview:
## Overview
This spec defines how Redwood Console surfaces **public status incidents** and **publishable post‑incident summaries** to logged-in customers, and how the UI handles **customer-specific context** without disclosing sensitive details (e.g., account-level capacity constraints, Dedicated cluster identifiers...
text:
## Overview
This spec defines how Redwood Console surfaces **public status incidents** and **publishable post‑incident summaries** to logged-in customers, and how the UI handles **customer-specific context** without disclosing sensitive details (e.g., account-level capacity constraints, Dedicated cluster identifiers, or customer-specific impact).

Console will not become a second incident-communications channel. It will:
1) link to the canonical public status page, and
2) provide lightweight “how this might affect you” context based on the customer’s recent usage and configured regions/capabilities.

Primary goals are improved transparency, reduced support burden during incidents, and better discoverability of post‑incident summaries.

---

## Goals
- **Discoverability:** Customers can find current incidents and recent post‑incident summaries from inside Console without searching exte...

title: SLO-backed Pricing & Bundled Capacity PRD
preview:
Overview

Problem statement:
Redwood customers pay for capacity and tokens, but many enterprise teams ask for predictable cost behavior tied to operational outcomes (latency SLOs, error budgets, and availability). Current unit-based pricing does not capture the value of: (1) guaranteed latency/availability for criti...
text:
Overview

Problem statement:
Redwood customers pay for capacity and tokens, but many enterprise teams ask for predictable cost behavior tied to operational outcomes (latency SLOs, error budgets, and availability). Current unit-based pricing does not capture the value of: (1) guaranteed latency/availability for critical routes, (2) bundled tooling (Optimize features, caching, and rollout controls), and (3) simpler accounting for reserved capacity + burst. This PRD proposes an SLO-backed pricing model and bundle constructs that map directly to customer SLAs and internal cost/accounting needs.

Objective (what success looks like)
- Launch 3 new bundled SKUs that combine reserved capacity, SLO guarantees, and optional Optimize features by Q2 2026.
- Achieve 20% uplift in MRR from existing Dedicated customers migrating to SLO Bundles within 12 months of GA.
- Reduce billing disputes for la...

title: Design Spec: Optimization Config Versioning + Safe Apply/Rollback
preview:
## Summary
This document specifies the end-to-end design for **Optimization Config versioning** and **safe apply/rollback workflows** across Hosted, Dedicated, and Private deployments.

Optimization Configs control runtime optimization behavior such as:
- batching heuristics
- caching toggles and parameters

text:
## Summary
This document specifies the end-to-end design for **Optimization Config versioning** and **safe apply/rollback workflows** across Hosted, Dedicated, and Private deployments.

Optimization Configs control runtime optimization behavior such as:
- batching heuristics
- caching toggles and parameters
- quantization profile selection

The design introduces immutable config versions, scoped rollouts with gating signals, and one-click rollback with strong auditability.

## Goals
1. **Immutable version history** for all optimization changes (who/what/when, diffable).
2. **Safe apply** via staged rollout (tenant/model/region scope) with gates:
 - SLO gates (5xx + p95/p99 latency)
 - saturation/capacity signals (GPU utilization, queueing)
 - eval/quality gates for quantization changes
3. **One-click rollback** (Console + API), with well-defined semantics in a distributed runtime.
4. ...

title: PRD: Model Metadata Schema v1 + Automated Changelog Generator
preview:
h1. Overview
Redwood Inference is expanding Unified Rollouts (canary + A/B testing) and model lifecycle tooling. Today, customers and internal teams cannot reliably answer: *what changed, why it changed, how risky it is, and how to roll back*. Model/version change details are spread across the model registry, runtim...
text:
h1. Overview
Redwood Inference is expanding Unified Rollouts (canary + A/B testing) and model lifecycle tooling. Today, customers and internal teams cannot reliably answer: *what changed, why it changed, how risky it is, and how to roll back*. Model/version change details are spread across the model registry, runtime configuration, PR descriptions, and ad-hoc release notes.

This PRD defines:
* A single, standardized *Model Metadata Schema v1* for all served models/variants/quantization profiles.
* An automated *changelog generator* that produces consistent, customer-visible release notes (Console + docs) and internal audit/change events.

h2. Problem Statement
h3. Current pain
* *Inconsistent customer visibility:* model updates can be promoted with incomplete or missing release notes (customer-facing “silent changes”).
* *No single source of truth:* model identity, versioning, compat...

title: Design Spec: Model Version Rollouts (Canonical Plan Template)
preview:

This page defines the canonical rollout plan template for introducing a new LLM model version behind an existing model name (alias) or introducing a new model endpoint that will receive production traffic. It is intended to be copied into a new rollout plan page per change.

h1. When this template is re...
text:
h1. Overview
This page defines the canonical rollout plan template for introducing a new LLM model version behind an existing model name (alias) or introducing a new model endpoint that will receive production traffic. It is intended to be copied into a new rollout plan page per change.

h1. When this template is required
Use this template for any of the following:
* Changing the default version behind a model alias
* Introducing a new version that will receive >0% external customer traffic
* Changing routing policy or fallback behavior in a way that affects customer requests

h1. Rollout plan template (copy/paste)

h2. 1) Summary
* What is changing?
* Why now?
* Expected customer impact (best-case and worst-case)
* Who is on point during rollout (name + Slack channel)

h2. 2) Scope
* Environments: Hosted / Dedicated / Private
* Regions impacted
* Customer tiers or allowlists (if any)...

title: Release Notes: Dedicated Autoscaling Bounds + Predictive Mode
preview:
h1. Dedicated Autoscaling Bounds + Predictive Mode (GA)

*Release date:* 2025-09-12

This release adds customer-configurable autoscaling guardrails for *Redwood Dedicated* deployments (hard *min/max capacity bounds*) and an optional *Predictive* mode that pre-scales for known traffic ramps while preserving safety in...
text:
h1. Dedicated Autoscaling Bounds + Predictive Mode (GA)

*Release date:* 2025-09-12

This release adds customer-configurable autoscaling guardrails for *Redwood Dedicated* deployments (hard *min/max capacity bounds*) and an optional *Predictive* mode that pre-scales for known traffic ramps while preserving safety invariants and auditability.

h2. What changed

h3. 1) Customer-configurable autoscaling bounds (hard enforcement)

You can now set *hard* per-deployment capacity bounds:

* *Min capacity* (baseline): autoscaler will not scale below this value.
* *Max capacity* (ceiling): autoscaler will not scale above this value.

Bounds are enforced in the autoscaler loop via deterministic clamping:

* We compute *requested desired capacity* (from reactive + optional predictive inputs).
* We compute *bounded desired capacity* = clamp(requested_desired, min, max).
* We persist/emit a reason...

title: Private Observability Minimum Pack — Product Requirements (v1)
preview:
## Summary
Redwood Private customers frequently ask “what’s the supported way to run observability?” Today, installs are inconsistent: some customers integrate Prometheus/Grafana and logs cleanly; others ship no dashboards, have incomplete scrapes, or produce unsafe/unstructured logs. This creates Support/SRE escala...
text:
## Summary
Redwood Private customers frequently ask “what’s the supported way to run observability?” Today, installs are inconsistent: some customers integrate Prometheus/Grafana and logs cleanly; others ship no dashboards, have incomplete scrapes, or produce unsafe/unstructured logs. This creates Support/SRE escalations and increases time-to-resolution.

The **Private observability minimum pack** defines and ships a **baseline, supported** observability bundle for Redwood Private installs (VPC and on-prem), packaged with the installer/Helm charts and validated in reference environments.

This page is the **v1 PRD**: personas, requirements, non-goals, and acceptance criteria.

---

## Goals
1. **Turnkey baseline observability** for Redwood Private installs with minimal customer configuration.
2. **Standardize** the minimum set of dashboards + alerts that Redwood Support/SRE will rely ...

text:
## Summary
This document specifies (1) the control plane schema and API surface for Dedicated autoscaling bounds and predictive scaling mode, and (2) the Redwood Console UX for configuring and observing these settings.

Primary goals:
- Let customers set hard, enforceable min/max capacity bounds per Dedicated deployment.
- Provide optional predictive scaling that biases desired capacity up ahead of known ramps while preserving safety and deterministic behavior.
- Preserve backward compatibility: no behavior change for existing deployments until explicitly enabled.

Non-goals (in this design):
- Cross-region predictive placement.
- Contract negotiation workflows.
- Fully automated procurement.

---

## Definitions
- **Deployment**: A Dedicated customer-facing serving deployment (model + configuration) with an autoscaled replica set.
- **Replica**: Unit of capacity for a deployment in t...

title: PRD: Dedicated Autoscaling Policies with Customer Bounds + Predictive Mode
preview:
## Summary
Dedicated customers want explicit control over scaling to (a) cap spend, (b) guarantee a baseline capacity for latency SLOs, and (c) prevent avoidable queueing during predictable daily/weekly ramps. This PRD defines customer-configurable autoscaling bounds (hard min/max) and an optional predictive mode th...
text:
## Summary
Dedicated customers want explicit control over scaling to (a) cap spend, (b) guarantee a baseline capacity for latency SLOs, and (c) prevent avoidable queueing during predictable daily/weekly ramps. This PRD defines customer-configurable autoscaling bounds (hard min/max) and an optional predictive mode that biases scaling ahead of expected demand, while preserving safety invariants and deterministic, auditable behavior.

## Background / Problem
Redwood Dedicated provides reserved GPU pools with predictable throughput and isolation. Today, autoscaling behavior is primarily platform-driven (reactive signals + operator overrides). This creates gaps for enterprise customers who:
- Need a contractual and operational “ceiling” to prevent unexpected spend (hard max).
- Need a “floor” to ensure baseline throughput/latency during business hours (hard min).
- Have repeatable traffic ...

title: Private VPC Terraform Modules (Alpha) — Release Notes

text:
## Summary
This page tracks the **Alpha** release notes for the **Redwood Private VPC Terraform modules** (AWS-only). The alpha module suite provides Redwood-validated defaults for the foundational networking and security footprint required to run **Redwood Private** in a customer-managed VPC (with EKS assumed in the reference architecture).

Alpha focus areas:
- Reduce pilot risk by standardizing **subnet layout**, **routing/NAT strategy**, **VPC endpoints**, and **logging defaults**.
- Provide a supported baseline for installer prerequisites (endpoints/DNS/egress) and predictable day-2 operations.
- Keep the surface area opinionated with documented extension points.

---

## Current Alpha Version
**Module suite version:** v0.1.0-alpha (initial tagged alpha)

**Support stance:** Alpha is supported for **design partners and guided pilots** only. Changes may be breaking between alpha v...

title: PRD: Private VPC Terraform Modules (Alpha)
preview:
## Summary
Redwood Private pilots repeatedly stall during technical discovery and early implementation due to variability and risk in customer AWS networking setups (VPC topology, subnets, routes/NAT, DNS behavior, VPC endpoints, logging destinations, security group boundaries, and IAM permissions for installer exec...
text:
## Summary
Redwood Private pilots repeatedly stall during technical discovery and early implementation due to variability and risk in customer AWS networking setups (VPC topology, subnets, routes/NAT, DNS behavior, VPC endpoints, logging destinations, security group boundaries, and IAM permissions for installer execution).

The **Private VPC Terraform Modules (Alpha)** initiative publishes a set of **opinionated, composable Terraform modules** that encode **Redwood-validated defaults** for a standard AWS private deployment footprint. The intent is to reduce time-to-pilot, minimize support escalations from misconfigured networking, and provide a repeatable baseline that can be extended in controlled ways.

This PRD defines the alpha scope, supported topologies, success metrics, rollout plan with design partners, and required documentation/artifacts.

---

### What ...

## Overview
This page specifies the public-facing web experience for Redwood Inference’s **Production Inference Economics Benchmark** launch:
- A **landing page** that communicates the narrative and methodology at a high level.
- An **interactive benchmark table** (with optional charts) for exploring results by model, region, and workload dimensions.
- A clear path to **download the PDF**, read the **methodology**, and convert via **Start free / Talk to an expert** CTAs.

This spec is written for Design + Web Engineering + PMM. It defines information architecture, interaction patterns, chart requirements, accessibility, and performance constraints. It intentionally does **not** redefine benchmark methodology; it references the canonical methodology doc.

## Goals / non-goals
### Goals
1. Make the benchmark results easy to understand and easy to compare across models and regions.
2. Co...

title: PRD: PLG-to-Sales Signal Routing + Playbook (Hosted → Dedicated/Private)
preview:
## Summary
Redwood Inference is PLG-led, but a growing share of expansion comes from sales-assisted upgrades to Dedicated and Private when customers hit scale, reliability, or compliance needs. Today, these moments are detected inconsistently (ad-hoc dashboards, anecdotal reports, manual CRM research), which creates...
text:
## Summary
Redwood Inference is PLG-led, but a growing share of expansion comes from sales-assisted upgrades to Dedicated and Private when customers hit scale, reliability, or compliance needs. Today, these moments are detected inconsistently (ad-hoc dashboards, anecdotal reports, manual CRM research), which creates two failure modes:
1) High-intent/high-need accounts are contacted too late (or not at all), missing Dedicated/Private conversion.
2) Low-signal/noise accounts get outreach that feels spammy, creating negative sentiment and support escalations.

This PRD defines a privacy-aware signal pipeline and a GTM follow-up playbook. The system detects product-led threshold + intent signals, enriches them with account context, scores/dedupes/suppresses them, and routes qualified signals into HubSpot with deterministic ownership and task creation.

## Problem statement
### What is bro...

title: PRD: SIEM-ready Audit Export (Beta)
preview:
## Summary
Enterprise customers increasingly treat Redwood audit logs as security evidence and incident-response inputs. Today, Redwood provides audit events in-console and via limited internal tooling, but customers cannot reliably export audit events into their SIEM / log analytics stack with enterprise-grade guar...
text:
## Summary
Enterprise customers increasingly treat Redwood audit logs as security evidence and incident-response inputs. Today, Redwood provides audit events in-console and via limited internal tooling, but customers cannot reliably export audit events into their SIEM / log analytics stack with enterprise-grade guarantees, filters, and operational visibility.

This PRD defines a **feature-flagged Beta** for **SIEM-ready audit export** that enables customers to export canonical Redwood audit events to:
- **Splunk** (HTTP Event Collector / HEC)
- **Datadog Logs** (HTTP intake)
- **Amazon S3** (object delivery for downstream ingestion)

The Beta emphasizes:
1) A stable, SIEM-friendly audit event schema (v2) with predictable identifiers
2) Configurable filtering to minimize noise and reduce sensitive data exposure
3) Delivery guarantees suitable for compliance and incident response (at-le...

title: Release Notes: Console Guided Onboarding + “First Production Launch” Checklist (2025-04)
preview:
## Summary
We shipped an in-product guided onboarding experience in the **Redwood Console**: a persistent **Onboarding Checklist** that walks a new tenant from first API key → first successful request → production-readiness basics (streaming, retries/timeouts, quotas/budgets, model pinning, and basic observability)....
text:
## Summary
We shipped an in-product guided onboarding experience in the **Redwood Console**: a persistent **Onboarding Checklist** that walks a new tenant from first API key → first successful request → production-readiness basics (streaming, retries/timeouts, quotas/budgets, model pinning, and basic observability).

This release is part of the “Guided Onboarding + First Production Launch” initiative to reduce:
- **TTFS** (time from signup to first successful API call)
- **TTFP** (time from signup to first production traffic)

The checklist is backed by a progress-tracking service and standardized server-side event instrumentation to make step completion reliable across SDKs and request patterns.

---

## What’s new
### 1) New: Onboarding Checklist in Console
**Where:** Console → **Getting Started** → **Onboarding checklist** (entry point also appears on the Console home page for elig...

Customers can safely introduce a new model version or configuration by gradually shifting traffic from a baseline to a canary cohort (e.g., 1% → 5% → 25% → 50% → 100%) with clear UI visibility, guardrails (step limits, soak time, approvals), automatic safety stops/rollbacks based on SLO signals, and auditable change history.

## Overview
This document defines the Console user experience and product behavior for configuring and operating **SIEM-ready Audit Export (Beta)**. It is intended for Product, Design, Console engineering, Control Plane engineering, Security, and SRE.

The feature enables enterprise customers to export Redwood audit events to external systems (initial beta: **Splunk HEC**, **Datadog Logs**, **Amazon S3**) with configurable filtering, backfill controls, and operational visibility.

This is a **product/UX-facing spec** (not an API contract). It assumes delivery semantics and filtering model are defined in ADRs and the canonical audit schema is defined in Security docs.

title: Dedicated Burst Capacity: Burst Pool Architecture & Guardrails (Design Spec)
preview:
## 1. Overview
This design specifies how Redwood Dedicated customers can temporarily exceed their reserved baseline GPU allocation by borrowing from Redwood-managed shared “burst pools,” while preserving platform-wide SLOs and protecting non-bursting tenants.

This document covers:
- Burst pool topology (per region ...
text:
## 1. Overview
This design specifies how Redwood Dedicated customers can temporarily exceed their reserved baseline GPU allocation by borrowing from Redwood-managed shared “burst pools,” while preserving platform-wide SLOs and protecting non-bursting tenants.

This document covers:
- Burst pool topology (per region and hardware class)
- Admission control and deterministic decisioning
- Fairness model for shared pools (token bucket / WFQ hybrid)
- Guardrails: per-tenant caps, cooldowns, global fleet safety, circuit breakers
- Routing/data plane integration and fallback behavior
- Failure modes and rollback strategy

Out of scope (initial release):
- Cross-region bursting (burst is constrained within the region)
- Customer-specific dedicated burst pools (initially shared Redwood-managed pools with strict segmentation)
- Market/spot style reservation trading

- **Baseli...

title: PRD: Hosted Canary Rollout Workflow (GA)
preview:

text:
## Summary
Redwood Inference is shipping a GA “Hosted canary rollout workflow” that allows customers to safely introduce a new model version and/or serving configuration on an existing *Hosted route* by gradually shifting traffic via percentage splits (e.g., 1% → 5% → 25% → 50% → 100%).

This PRD covers the customer workflow in Redwood Console, the underlying rollout orchestration model, routing enforcement, telemetry/stop conditions, auditability, and the GA bar.

## Problem statement
Customers operating production LLM workloads need a safe and auditable path to change model versions and serving configs (quantization, prompt templates, tool/function calling settings, safety filters, etc.). Today, most customers rely on ad-hoc approaches:
- “Big bang” route updates with manual rollback when issues occur.
- Parallel routes and client-side traffic splitting.
- Custom scripts to change m...

text:
## 1. Overview
This document specifies the end-to-end user experience for **Hosted canary rollouts** in Redwood Console (GA scope): creating a rollout, progressing traffic steps, pausing, requesting/receiving approvals, and rolling back. It includes screen-by-screen behavior, validation rules, empty/error states, and the key UI-to-backend interactions.

Hosted canary rollouts enable customers to safely introduce a new model version/config on a **Hosted route** by gradually shifting a percentage of traffic from a **baseline** (current production) to a **canary** (new version/config), with policy guardrails and automatic stop/rollback based on SLO signals.

### 1.1 Goals
- Provide a **GA-ready wizard** to create a canary rollout on a Hosted route.
- Provide a **Rollout details** experience that supports: start, step forward, pause/resume, rollback, and approvals.

title: Runtime LTS channel launch brief (Product-facing)
preview:

Runtime LTS is an enterprise-friendly Long Term Support channel for the Redwood Serving Runtime (kernels + scheduling) that prioritizes stability, predictable patch cadence, and auditable artifacts over rapid feature delivery.

This brief provides product-facing positioning, customer eligibility, external...
text:
## Summary
Runtime LTS is an enterprise-friendly Long Term Support channel for the Redwood Serving Runtime (kernels + scheduling) that prioritizes stability, predictable patch cadence, and auditable artifacts over rapid feature delivery.

This brief provides product-facing positioning, customer eligibility, external/internal communication plan, and the end-user experience in Console and Docs.

**Why now:** Dedicated and Private customers are increasingly blocked by release churn, performance variance, and urgent incident-driven upgrades. LTS provides a stable baseline with explicit backport policy and repeatable release artifacts that meet regulated change-control expectations.

---

## Customer promise (what we are committing to)
### What LTS is
- A **stable runtime baseline** supported for **6–12+ months** (per LTS line).
- A **predictable patch cadence**: **monthly patches**, plus ...

title: PRD: Route-level A/B Testing Framework (Console Rollouts)
preview:
## Summary
Route-level A/B Testing ("Route Experiments") extends the Redwood Console Rollouts surface area to support A/B/n testing at the request-routing layer. Customers and internal teams will be able to define experiments per route/endpoint and traffic policy, deterministically assign requests to variants, and m...
text:
## Summary
Route-level A/B Testing ("Route Experiments") extends the Redwood Console Rollouts surface area to support A/B/n testing at the request-routing layer. Customers and internal teams will be able to define experiments per route/endpoint and traffic policy, deterministically assign requests to variants, and measure variant performance directly in Console using first-class hooks for latency, cost, error rates, throughput, and (optionally) eval-based quality signals.

This PRD covers personas, core workflows, guardrails, success metrics, and the phased rollout plan from beta to GA.

## Problem statement
Teams want to validate changes (model versions, quantization profiles, batching/caching settings, prompt templates, tool-calling settings) safely in production. Today, the closest alternative is a combination of:
- Canary rollouts that primarily validate operational safety, but do...

preview:

This document proposes Redwood Dedicated pricing/packaging for v1 tiered throughput guarantees. It maps (a) tier entitlements (throughput floor + burst + failover posture + support SLAs) to (b) standardized hardware “shapes” by region and (c) list price guidance + discount guardrails.

This is intended...
text:
## 1) Summary
This document proposes Redwood Dedicated pricing/packaging for v1 tiered throughput guarantees. It maps (a) tier entitlements (throughput floor + burst + failover posture + support SLAs) to (b) standardized hardware “shapes” by region and (c) list price guidance + discount guardrails.

This is intended to be:
- Consistent with the Dedicated Throughput SLO definition (metric semantics, exclusions, and reporting).
- Operationally manageable (standard shapes, predictable headroom, tier-aware routing/admission control).
- Quoteable by Sales with minimal bespoke sizing work.

**Non-goals (v1):** bespoke per-customer kernel/quantization tuning commitments in the commercial package; guaranteeing quality metrics; guaranteeing throughput across arbitrary model mixes without pinning/model constraints.

## 1. Overview
This document defines the Redwood Console user experience for RBAC v2 role management. It covers:
- Viewing and searching permissions (permission viewer)
- Creating and editing roles (role editor)
- Using built-in role templates
- Assigning roles via bindings (users, groups, service accounts) at a defined scope
- “Why was I denied?” guidance patterns for Console actions

The design prioritizes least-privilege enterprise workflows and aligns with deny-by-default enforcement.

### 1.1 Goals
1) Enable security admins to safely manage access without needing API-only workflows.
2) Make it difficult to accidentally over-grant privileges (guardrails, clarity, warnings).
3) Provide fast, debuggable permission-denied experiences with actionable next steps.
4) Support Hosted, Dedicated, and Private semantics consistently, with scope-aware UX.

title: Design Spec: Staged rollouts for Dedicated pools (reserved capacity)
preview:
## Summary
This document describes the end-to-end design for **Dedicated staged rollouts for reserved pools**. The feature introduces explicit rollout plans with operator/customer-visible stages and **objective gates** (latency, errors, saturation/headroom, GPU health, OOM/evictions, request drops/429s, and optional...
text:
## Summary
This document describes the end-to-end design for **Dedicated staged rollouts for reserved pools**. The feature introduces explicit rollout plans with operator/customer-visible stages and **objective gates** (latency, errors, saturation/headroom, GPU health, OOM/evictions, request drops/429s, and optional quality checks where applicable).

The design spans:
- **Control plane** (data model + API + audit)
- **Routing/data plane** (partitioning and request assignment by stage)
- **Policy engine** (gate evaluation and deterministic decisions)
- **Telemetry/observability** (stage-tagged signals, dashboards, alerting)
- **Console** (timeline UI, promotion/abort/hold, gate evidence drilldowns)

This is *not* a general-purpose experimentation platform. It supports A/B only insofar as it is used for safe, gated rollouts.

---

## Goals and non-goals
### Goals
1) Provide a first-clas...

## 1. Summary
This document specifies the Redwood Console UX for creating, operating, and reviewing **route-level experiments** (A/B/n tests) in the Rollouts surface area. The experience is designed to be safe-by-default (guardrails + clear blast radius), RBAC-aware, and metrics-forward.

Route-level experiments let users split traffic for a specific **route/endpoint** across multiple **variants** (typically model versions or compatible model IDs), deterministically assign requests to variants, and review results (latency, cost, errors, throughput, and optional quality/eval signals).

## 2. Scope
### In scope (this UX spec)
- Experiments list + entry points
- Experiment creation wizard

 - Deterministic assignment / stickiness key selection

 - Guardrails configuration (latency/cost/error + optional quality)

title: PRD: Dedicated staged rollouts for reserved pools
preview:
## Summary
Dedicated customers rely on reserved GPU pools for predictable throughput and isolation. Today, upgrading a Dedicated pool (new model version, runtime build, routing policy, or config change) is higher risk than Hosted because it can impact all traffic for that customer at once.

This PRD defines “Dedicat...
text:
## Summary
Dedicated customers rely on reserved GPU pools for predictable throughput and isolation. Today, upgrading a Dedicated pool (new model version, runtime build, routing policy, or config change) is higher risk than Hosted because it can impact all traffic for that customer at once.

This PRD defines “Dedicated staged rollouts for reserved pools”: a first-class, auditable rollout plan that partitions a Dedicated pool into explicit stages (e.g., 1%/10%/50%/100% or replica partitions) with measurable gates required before promotion. Rollouts must be deterministic, reversible, and consistent across regions and failure modes.

## Background / Customer problem
### What is failing today
Dedicated pool changes currently happen via one of:
- Manual, operator-driven changes with implicit canarying (ad hoc)
- Coarse “flip” changes where the new version receives all traffic
- Customer cha...

title: PRD: Cache Hit-Rate Recommendations MVP (Console/Optimize + Internal SRE)
preview:

1) **Internal operators (SRE/Runtime/Platform)** to speed triage and reduce cost regressions.
2) A minimal **Console/Optimize surface** (internal-first...
text:
## Overview
This PRD defines the **MVP** for turning new prefix/KV cache telemetry into **actionable “increase hit-rate” recommendations** for two initial audiences:
1) **Internal operators (SRE/Runtime/Platform)** to speed triage and reduce cost regressions.
2) A minimal **Console/Optimize surface** (internal-first, optionally customer-visible for early design partners) that validates whether the recommendations are understandable and drive measurable improvements.

This MVP is intentionally scoped to **safe, high-confidence suggestions** that map directly to existing levers (configuration and operational practices). We are not shipping a full customer-facing optimization product in this sprint.

---

## Problem statement
Cache hit-rate and cache effectiveness vary materially by:
- model family + version
- route configuration (chat vs completion, streaming vs non-streaming)
- request...

title: PRD: Quantization Profile Library (Opt-in, Route-level Enablement)
preview:

Redwood Inference will launch an *opt-in Quantization Profile Library* that publishes *per-model, versioned quantization profiles* and enables customers to turn them on *per route* (and optionally scoped by region and/or deployment tier) to reduce inference cost while preserving quality and meeting laten...
text:
h1. Summary
Redwood Inference will launch an *opt-in Quantization Profile Library* that publishes *per-model, versioned quantization profiles* and enables customers to turn them on *per route* (and optionally scoped by region and/or deployment tier) to reduce inference cost while preserving quality and meeting latency SLOs.

This PRD defines user stories, product scope, API + Console requirements, rollout plan, and success metrics for v1.

h1. Goals (v1)
*G1. Stable, versioned profile schema per model variant*
* Profiles are stored in the model registry with validation and compatibility metadata.
* Profiles are versioned and immutable once published.

*G2. Safe route-level enablement controls*
* Customers can enable/disable a specific profile per route.
* Optional scoping by *region* and/or *deployment tier* (Hosted, Dedicated, Private) where supported.
* Changes are auditable and rev...

title: PRD: Baseline Compliance Reporting Pack (On-demand Exports + Customer Package)
preview:

The Baseline Compliance Reporting Pack is a standardized, customer-ready set of exports and summaries that Redwood Inference can generate on-demand to accelerate enterprise security reviews and support internal audits. The pack focuses on the minimum evidence that repeatedly blocks deals: audit log covera...
text:
## Summary
The Baseline Compliance Reporting Pack is a standardized, customer-ready set of exports and summaries that Redwood Inference can generate on-demand to accelerate enterprise security reviews and support internal audits. The pack focuses on the minimum evidence that repeatedly blocks deals: audit log coverage and exports, retention configuration, data residency/region posture, and access control/RBAC posture.

This PRD defines the pack contents, customer expectations (what it proves / what it does not), integrations (audit-log-exporter + telemetry), output formats and versioning, and MVP scope for the initial GA.

## Problem statement
Enterprise prospects adopting Dedicated and Private deployments consistently request:
- Proof that Redwood maintains audit logs for key administrative and access events.
- A time-windowed export of audit events for their review.
- Clear retentio...

title: PRD: RBAC v2 (Enterprise Controls)
preview:
## Summary
RBAC v2 is Redwood Inference’s next-generation authorization layer designed for regulated enterprise workloads across Hosted, Dedicated, and Private deployments. RBAC v2 provides least-privilege access control with deny-by-default enforcement, consistent permissions across product surfaces, scoped role bi...
text:
## Summary
RBAC v2 is Redwood Inference’s next-generation authorization layer designed for regulated enterprise workloads across Hosted, Dedicated, and Private deployments. RBAC v2 provides least-privilege access control with deny-by-default enforcement, consistent permissions across product surfaces, scoped role bindings (org/project/environment/cluster), and auditability of authorization decisions for SOC2 evidence and enterprise security questionnaires.

This PRD defines: (1) the customer problem and target personas, (2) functional requirements for the platform, API Gateway, control plane, and Console, (3) success metrics and guardrails, and (4) a phased release plan with migration and compatibility constraints.

## Problem statement
Redwood’s existing authorization model (RBAC v1 and/or mixed service-local checks) is insufficient for enterprise buyers who require:
- Least-privileg...

The checklist makes the critical steps from signup → first successful API call → first production traffic explicit, trackable, and easier to complete.

## Overview
This document defines the metrics, telemetry events, and traffic tagging required for the **Quickstart Templates v1** launch (RAG + Agents templates across Python and TypeScript). The goal is to measure adoption, activation, and ongoing retention while respecting privacy constraints and avoiding high-cardinality analytics.

**Primary use cases**
- Measure funnel from docs/CLI to “first successful request”.
- Attribute API traffic to template-generated apps (opt-in) to assess retention and quality.
- Quantify support deflection (lower ticket rate / faster time-to-resolution) over time.

**Non-goals (v1)**
- User-level session replay, prompt content capture, or any collection of customer data beyond minimal operational/attribution fields.
- Perfect causal attribution across all acquisition channels (we will prioritize docs → CLI → API request instrumentation).

## Summary
This document specifies the **Redwood Console** experience for configuring and validating **Retention Controls** and for initiating/downloading baseline **Compliance Reporting Pack** artifacts.

The Retention Settings page is the primary UI for:
- Viewing current **effective retention** by data class (and by region when supported)
- Editing retention within tier guardrails (min/max TTL) with clear warnings and prerequisites
- Understanding and exporting **change history** for retention settings
- Downloading exports required for security reviews (retention config export, audit-log templates link) and initiating a **Compliance Reporting Pack** generation

This is a UX and interaction spec; exact schema validation is owned by the retention policy service and documented in `retention-policy-schema-and-validation`.

title: PRD: Retention Controls (Enterprise Security & Compliance Foundation)
preview:
## 1. Summary
Retention Controls productize customer-configurable data retention across core Redwood Inference data classes (request/response logs, prompts/completions where stored, audit logs, traces/metrics, and limited artifacts) with safe defaults per deployment tier. The feature set includes:
- A consistent ret...
text:
## 1. Summary
Retention Controls productize customer-configurable data retention across core Redwood Inference data classes (request/response logs, prompts/completions where stored, audit logs, traces/metrics, and limited artifacts) with safe defaults per deployment tier. The feature set includes:
- A consistent retention policy model, evaluated centrally, with tier-aware validation.
- Enforcement mechanisms (TTL + purge jobs) for supported stores with verification metrics.
- Change history and exportability for customer security reviews and internal audits.

This PRD covers the Retention Controls component of the broader “Retention controls + compliance reporting pack” initiative.

## 2. Problem statement
Enterprise and regulated customers increasingly require:
- Explicit retention periods per data class (and sometimes per region).
- Clear deletion semantics (TTL vs. purge, time-to-d...
